children.text: "Carita Linda" is another bomba fusion work, released in 2025, produced with bomba practitioners from La Familia Cepeda such as Exan Cepeda (Ortiz), "Chino" Rafael Cepeda, and Roberto Cepeda. The video itself, in contrast to "Besito en la Frente," specifically highlights locality by displaying famous areas of Loíza such as the kiosks where people cook and consume alcapurria or traditional Afro-Puerto Rican food made with fried codfish. It also features the Loíza Aldea Museo de Arte de Puerto Rico, vejigantes, and community bombazos in Loíza. The video also constitutes some confusion in displaying persons playing soccer instead of béisbol, which is considered more of Puerto Rico’s national sport, albeit soccer has been becoming more popular across pan-Latino communities at large. Additionally, the scenes involving horses—specifically paso fino, a breed imported to Puerto Rico from Spain—are notable. These horses are commonly seen roaming freely across the northeastern adjacent islands off the coast of Puerto Rico, such as Vieques and Culebra.

The elements that did not appear visibly nationalist were Rauw Alejandro’s outfits and the choreography chosen, whose footwork appears more akin to flamenco than bomba, though visually aligned with the scene of the paso fino horses Rauw rode in the film while performing. Scenes of typical quotidian or vernacular life are common in Puerto Rico, and bomba scenes are constantly interjected throughout. This video is also significant in that it was the first to be termed "neo-bomba" by a periodical known as MSN, in an article titled Carita Linda: A Love Letter to Puerto Rico. Hector [apellido] expressed dislike for the term "neo-bomba," believing it was too confusing for the public, not clear enough, and detracted from the history of bomba as a tradition. He argued that it did not make sense because these commercial collaborations, although not totally out of the ordinary for bomba, had precedents. For example, Ismael Rivera and El Gran Combo had done salsa-bomba fusions in the [add decade]. It still did not make sense to hyphenate it.36

In November 2025, Margarita Caridad "Cary" Martinez-Sanchez de la Familia Cepeda performed alongside Rauw Alejandro for "Besito en la frente" in its official extended video form. It is significant that over half of the video defines bomba as a genre and its cultural significance in a poetic manner. See narration below:

La bomba no es solo música, es la tierra hecha sonido, un latido que llegó de África y floreció en Puerto Rico. Un diálogo entre tambor, cuerpo y la voz colectiva, cada paso y cada golpe en el tambor nos recuerda que estamos conectados con el pasado y con el futuro. Una tradición nacida de la resistencia, que hoy sigue siendo una celebración de la libertad. Cada pueblo de Puerto Rico expresa la bomba a su manera. Los ritmos laten en la arena, un diálogo improvisado donde el cuerpo ordena y la música obedece. Los ritmos se mezclan con el carnaval y la energía del pueblo. Los vejigantes, mitad enigmáticos, tallados por artesanos y pintados con colores vibrantes, no son disfraces, son espíritus del Caribe. Despiertan la memoria ancestral de nuestro pueblo, son guardianes de la cultura, símbolos de resistencia que encarnan la eterna batalla contra lo oscuro. En cada máscara arde la fuerza de un pueblo que transforma el miedo en poder y la lucha en celebración. El baile de bomba es fuerza, tensión y libertad. La bomba es unión: une a pueblos y a generaciones, orgullo cultural Afro-Boricua. Cuando el ritmo llama, toda la comunidad responde. This introduction to the song is especially significant because it reveals a larger difference in artistic strategies compared to Bad Bunny in terms of authenticity. As Hector has stated, "Rauw put in the work" to know bomba in a way closer to the community than Benito. He attended local bomba hubs such as Terraza de Bonanza, a chinchorro in San Juan known for hosting bailes de bomba, along with rumba, salsa, and plena. Rauw has also been taking classes directly from Jesús Cepeda and Mario Cepeda. His dedication to learning how to dance bomba is therefore more authentic and meaningful than the Bad Bunny collaboration, even though the Bad Bunny collaboration had a greater impact in regard to the international reception of bomba and in terms of total economic revenue generated for practitioners.

"Cultura" is a bomba-heavy metal fusion piece that was released in 2024. For this project, Puya recruited members of El Tribu, and also included José Emmanueli of the distinguished Emmanueli brothers (Vitito, José, and Julio). The video also incorporated vejigantes, though in a morbid and unconventional manner that has been analyzed by heavy metal scholars [add citation here]. Despite the innovative blending of genres, the work did not achieve significant impact. While Puya has enjoyed popularity both in Puerto Rico and internationally, and is recognized for its experimentation with local Puerto Rican genres, narratives, and lyrics within the framework of metal and rock, the "Cultura" itself was not widely disseminated within the bomba community. In fact, I personally publicized its release through my academic social media account @ethnmusicologypr to raise awareness of its existence. Nevertheless, it represents another example of bomba circulating in new contexts and contributing to the discourse surrounding neo-bomba. Members of the bomba community responded positively to the effort, yet ultimately emphasized the cultural and economic influence of Rauw Alejandro and Bad Bunny, whose collaborations have had far greater resonance in shaping both the reception and the transnational visibility of bomba.

“De Carolina”37 was created by Reggaetonero Rauw Alejandro and DJ Playero, and it celebrates both urbanity and street culture in Carolina, Puerto Rico. Its significance lies in the fact that, in 2024, the Delegación del Carolina38 employed the introduction of the song at the Encuentro de Tambores before transitioning into a more traditional bomba style. This moment of paraphrase, or splicing, resembles practices in hip-hop and rap, where short melodic segments or lyrics are interjected into a new song to allude to a shared musical legacy. It is also important because it highlights how commercial collaborations are giving rise to a new subset of the genre, often referred to as neo-bomba, which fuses traditional bomba with commercial music. The bomba interpretation of “De Carolina” differs by shifting into a distinct chorus, yet it demonstrates how the younger generation is innovating within the genre. While this innovation risks losing sight of bomba’s “fundamentals,” it enriches the tradition by infusing youthful creativity. In doing so, it continues to celebrate places and acknowledge humble roots. This moment of musical overlap also shows that bomba practitioners not only collaborate with commercial music partners but also allow these shared spaces and collaborations to shape their own sense of musical experimentation and identity in profound new ways. Beyond this, this song also shows that Rauw Alejandro occupies a more 'authentic' place within the bomba community, than Bad Bunny. Rauw, unlike Bad Bunny, can be found dancing bomba in local spaces, such as [Terranza Bonanza and El Iman]. It's one thing to perform with bomberos, but its another to learn besides bomba elders.
